metric_value: $101.8 million (46% increase over prior year)
notes: Fiscal year ending May 31; joined over-$100M club in FY1996

metric_value: Over 2,300 customers
notes: Across AS/400, Unix, and Windows NT operating environments

metric_value: Over 800 employees
notes: 14 North American offices; UK subsidiary; 7 international distributors; expanding international affiliates

metric_value: Process Orientation — cross-functional business processes via Lawson Process Suites (Financials, HR, Procurement, Supply Chain)
notes: Replaces functional silo orientation; based on BPR learning that operations are cross-functional

metric_value: Event-based Management — Activity-Based Management with financial and process data for decision support
notes: ABM implemented via Activity Repository (intelligent data warehouse synchronized with GL)

metric_value: Continual Improvement — multi-level cross-organizational workflow enabling process evolution
notes: Workflow encompasses personal, collaborative (workgroup), and enterprise capabilities

metric_value: Ubiquitous Information Access — Drill-Around analysis, OLAP, web-deployable WebDesktop and Web Service Centers
notes: Lawson's trademark Drill-Around analysis; WebDesktop in second release by May 1997

metric_value: Second release; full BMS functionality from browser; Insight screens as JAVA forms; supports JavaScript and Microsoft Office integration
notes: Phase II to add JAVA Server support for true dynamic B2B and B2C electronic commerce

metric_value: Activity Repository as intelligent data warehouse: synchronized with GL yet unconstrained by GL metadata model; avoids GL performance degradation
notes: Contrasted with competitors who subvert GL with ABC data — simplistic but inflexible

metric_value: 6 pre-built web apps: Employee Service Center, Supervisor Service Center, Internal Procurement Center, Customer Information Center, Vendor Information Center, Financial Information Center
notes: Ready-to-go pre-built web applications for immediate Internet business benefits

metric_value: Maintains business object methods shared across multiple application suites; combined with multi-level workflow for process models
notes: Foundation of Version 7.0 process-centric architecture; enables cross-organizational process flexibility

metric_value: Go-up-market strategy: non-manufacturing process-oriented EBA + vertical market focus (healthcare, multi-tier retail, hospitality, publishing)
notes: Healthcare vertical 'first and highly successful'; 1997 diversification to retail, hospitality, publishing

metric_value: Best-of-breed EBA now horizontally defined as business process (e.g., Procurement) rather than vertically by function (e.g., Accounts Payable)
notes: Aberdeen describes fundamental shift in how enterprises evaluate and select EBA

metric_name: Internet as first business context for OO
metric_value: The Internet will provide the first business context for widespread adoption of object-oriented line-of-business solutions
notes: Aberdeen prediction about Internet accelerating OO adoption; network computing architecture shift

metric_name: Aberdeen competitive assessment of Lawson vs. enterprise rivals
metric_value: Lawson ahead of SAP, Oracle, PeopleSoft on process orientation, event-based management, continual improvement, and ubiquitous information access
notes: Aberdeen notes enterprise suppliers like Oracle, PeopleSoft, SAP are 'getting surprised' by Lawson

metric_value: Lawson stands out as a company able to continually reinvent itself, its business solutions, its integration of leading-edge technology and its market value proposition
notes: Repeated in both preface and conclusions — Aberdeen's core thesis about Lawson

metric_name: Aberdeen on Lawson healthcare vertical success
metric_value: Healthcare initiative begun nearly three years ago is an excellent example of Lawson's vertical focus and drive-to-dominate
notes: Healthcare market keenly aware of need for process-based best practices

metric_name: Lawson revenue growth vs. EBA peers
metric_value: 46% YoY revenue growth to $101.8M in FY1996; crossed $100M threshold ahead of most midrange peers
notes: Aberdeen characterizes this as joining the '$100-million-and-up-club'

metric_value: Migrated to open systems in late 1980s; nearly a decade more experience than midrange rivals (JD Edwards, Ross, SSA) in advanced distributable multi-tier technologies
notes: Lawson's first open systems implementation in 1989 (per Financials section)

metric_value: Lawson is leading its competitors in anticipating the Internet watershed event and delivering products allowing enterprises to gain advantage through advanced technology + flexible process orientation
notes: Aberdeen's forward-looking competitive positioning statement for Lawson

metric_value: 1997 vertical strategy will diversify to multi-tier retail, hospitality and leisure, and publishing markets
notes: After healthcare vertical success; Aberdeen implies high probability of success given healthcare track record

metric_value: Internet will provide the first business context for widespread adoption of OO line-of-business solutions; enterprises will accelerate acquisitions of inherently Internet-enabled solutions
notes: Aberdeen prediction about internet-native EBA becoming the norm

metric_value: Acquired by Infor and Golden Gate Capital for $2B in 2012 after going public ~2001; had grown to significant healthcare ERP market share before acquisition
notes: Source: GovTech https://www.govtech.com/archive/Lawson-Software-Going-Private-in-2-Billion-Deal.html; TechCrunch 2011; Golden Gate Capital press release 2012

metric_value: Acquired by Oracle in hostile takeover for $10.3B in 2005; PeopleSoft brand eventually discontinued
notes: Oracle's controversial hostile bid began 2003; completed January 2005

metric_value: Acquired by PeopleSoft for $1.7B in 2003; then absorbed into Oracle via 2005 hostile takeover; continues as Oracle JD Edwards EnterpriseOne
notes: JD Edwards was Lawson's primary midrange competitor; acquisition confirmed Lawson's superior independent trajectory

metric_name: Internet as ERP delivery platform — actual outcome
metric_value: SaaS/cloud ERP (Salesforce, Workday, NetSuite) achieved exactly the internet-native enterprise app delivery model Aberdeen predicted; Workday founded 2005; cloud ERP became dominant by 2015
notes: Aberdeen's 1997 prediction about internet-native enterprise applications proved prescient; specific timing was slower than predicted

metric_value: Acquired by Microsoft for $1.1B in 2001; became Microsoft Business Solutions; now Microsoft Dynamics GP
notes: Desktop competitor Aberdeen identified; Microsoft acquisition validated their SMB focus
